stem: Evaluate the iterated integral \( \int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]} \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left( [[p]]x + [[q]]y \right) \, dx \, dy \).
solution: Integrate in \(x\) first, treating \(y\) as constant: \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dx = [[p]]\frac{[[b]]^2-[[a]]^2}{2} + [[q]]([[b]]-[[a]])\,y \). Then integrate this in \(y\) from \([[c]]\) to \([[d]]\) to obtain \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the \(y\)-term instead of adding it (a sign error on the second piece).
This treats the integrand as the constant \([[p]]+[[q]]\) times the area of the rectangle, forgetting to integrate \(x\) and \(y\).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the iterated integral \( \int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]} \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} [[k]]\,x^{[[m]]} y^{[[n]]} \, dx \, dy \), where the integrand is a single separable monomial in \(x\) and \(y\).
solution: Because the integrand is a single product \(x^{[[m]]}y^{[[n]]}\), the double integral SEPARATES into a product of two single-variable integrals: \( [[k]]\left(\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}x^{[[m]]}dx\right)\left(\int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]}y^{[[n]]}dy\right) = [[k]]\cdot\dfrac{[[b]]^{[[m]]+1}-[[a]]^{[[m]]+1}}{[[m]]+1}\cdot\dfrac{[[d]]^{[[n]]+1}-[[c]]^{[[n]]+1}}{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the power rule's denominator entirely, using \(x^{[[m]]}\) and \(y^{[[n]]}\) evaluated at the endpoints instead of their antiderivatives.
This applies the antiderivative power rule to the WIDTH of each interval (\(b-a\), \(d-c\)) rather than to the endpoints themselves.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the iterated integral \( \int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]} \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left( [[p]]xy + [[q]]x^2 + [[r]]y^2 \right) \, dx \, dy \), whose integrand mixes a cross term \(xy\) with pure single-variable terms.
solution: Split by linearity into three iterated integrals. The cross term separates as \( [[p]]\left(\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}x\,dx\right)\left(\int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]}y\,dy\right) \); each pure term integrates over BOTH variables (e.g. \( [[q]]x^2 \) integrates in \(x\) then is multiplied by the \(y\)-width \([[d]]-[[c]]\)). Summing the three pieces gives \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This treats every term as if it depended on only one variable, using interval widths instead of the correct antiderivative powers.
This uses the wrong endpoints (interval widths raised to a power) for the pure \(x^2\) and \(y^2\) terms instead of the difference of cubes/squares at the actual bounds.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Find the AVERAGE VALUE of \( f(x,y) = [[p]]x + [[q]]y \) over the rectangle \( [[a]] \le x \le [[b]],\ [[c]] \le y \le [[d]] \), i.e. \( \dfrac{1}{\text{Area}} \displaystyle\int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]} \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x,y) \, dx \, dy \).
solution: The average value is \( \dfrac{1}{[[Area]]}\int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]}\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} ([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dx\,dy \). Since \(f\) is linear, this equals \(f\) evaluated at the CENTER of the rectangle: \( [[p]]\cdot\dfrac{[[a]]+[[b]]}{2} + [[q]]\cdot\dfrac{[[c]]+[[d]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the raw (un-averaged) double integral, forgetting to divide by the area of the rectangle.
This uses the midpoint formula but adds a stray \(+1\).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the iterated integral \( \int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]} \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left( [[p]]\,|x| + [[q]]y \right) \, dx \, dy \), where the \(x\)-interval \([[a]],[[b]]\) straddles \(0\).
solution: Since \(|x|\) changes formula at \(x=0\), split \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} |x|\,dx = \int_{[[a]]}^{0}(-x)\,dx + \int_{0}^{[[b]]} x\,dx = \dfrac{[[a]]^2+[[b]]^2}{2} \). Multiplying by the \(y\)-width and adding the (ordinary) \(y\)-term contribution gives \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This integrates \(x\) directly (ignoring the absolute value) using \((b^2-a^2)/2\), which is wrong-signed since \(a<0\).
This uses \(a+b\) (the signed endpoints) instead of \(a^2+b^2\), as if \(|x|\) were just \(x\) shifted.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
